original_abstract:
The optical properties of biomass-burning organic carbon (OC) undergo significant transformations during atmospheric aging, yet the governing factors remain poorly understood. This study investigates the evolution of light absorption for smoldering wood smoke aerosol photochemically aged in a $104\,\text{m}^3$ outdoor dual chamber under natural sunlight. Using a UV-visible spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere, we observed a dynamic diurnal pattern in the mass absorption cross section (MAC) of OC. In most cases, the integrated MAC ($280–600\,\text{nm}$) increased by 11–54% during the morning hours, likely due to the formation of secondary chromophores from phenolic precursors, before declining by 19–68% in the afternoon. 

High relative humidity (RH) was found to accelerate this bleaching process, whereas elevated $\text{NO}_x$ levels slightly sustained the aerosol color, suggesting that $\text{NO}_x$ promotes the formation of resilient nitrogen-containing chromophores. Chemical characterization revealed a rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity and levoglucosan concentrations over time, indicating the degradation of primary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and sugar markers. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy demonstrated that while the aerosol became more oxygenated, its hygroscopicity decreased with aging. These findings, corroborated by ambient data collected during a major Florida wildfire, suggest that biomass-burning OC overall becomes less light-absorbing after approximately 8–9 hours of solar exposure. This dynamic evolution of "brown carbon" absorptivity should be accounted for in radiative forcing models to more accurately assess the climate impact of biomass combustion.
humanized_abstract:
Despite recent advances, it is still poorly understood which parameters control the optical evolution of biomass-burning organic carbon (OC). This study investigates how light absorption changes with photochemical aging of aerosol produced from smoldering wood smoke in a $104\,\text{m}^3$ outdoor dual chamber facility. Light absorption measurements of photochemically aged aerosol were made using a UV-visible spectrometer coupled with an integrating sphere. An increase of 11–54% in the integrated mass absorption cross section (MAC; $280–600\,\text{nm}$) relative to its initial value is observed in most cases during the morning. Possible explanations include secondary chromophores formed photochemically from phenolic species such as phenol and guaiacol, which are emitted with large amounts of OC in wood smoke. Such secondary chromophores are observed to lead to a MAC decrease of up to 68% relative to its morning value during the afternoon.

Although fluorescence yields provided a direct indication of their content of absorbing chromophores, the measured fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3) and levoglucosan (Fig. S3) decreased rapidly as OC was subjected to photo-oxidation in the Tropox lab chamber. The effect of RH was explored, and high RH accelerated this bleaching effect while $\text{NO}_x$ enriched air had only a slight positive effect on aerosol color suggesting that $\text{NO}_x}$ is important in forming more stable nitrogen-containing chromophores. The decrease in fluorescence intensity and levoglucosan along with little change to the fluorescence spectra implied that primary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and other sugar markers that excite at shorter wavelengths are rapidly degraded in a photo-oxidation environment, whereas FTIR demonstrated the OC aerosol was becoming more oxygenated, and hygroscopicity decreased as the aerosol aged. These lab studies, which are strongly supported by a coincident biomass burning (BB)OC characterization from an extensive wildfire in southern Florida in July-August 2011, confirm that after several hours of photo-oxidation, the predominantly BC constituent in BB OC contributes much less to light absorption that is attributed to "brown carbon", which therefore must be accounted for in assessments of radiative forcing arising from BB.

document.output:
Despite recent advances, it is still poorly understood which parameters control the optical evolution of biomass-burning organic carbon (OC). This study investigates how light absorption changes with photochemical aging of aerosol produced from smoldering wood smoke in a $104\,\text{m}^3$ outdoor dual chamber facility. Although fluorescence yields provided a direct indication of their content of absorbing chromophores, the measured fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3) and levoglucosan (Fig. S3) decreased rapidly as OC was subjected to photo-oxidation in the Tropox lab chamber. document.input:
The optical properties of biomass-burning organic carbon (OC) undergo significant transformations during atmospheric aging, yet the governing factors remain poorly understood. This study investigates the evolution of light absorption for smoldering wood smoke aerosol photochemically aged in a $104\,\text{m}^3$ outdoor dual chamber under natural sunlight. High relative humidity (RH) was found to accelerate this bleaching process, whereas elevated $\text{NO}_x$ levels slightly sustained the aerosol color, suggesting that $\text{NO}_x$ promotes the formation of resilient nitrogen-containing chromophores. Chemical characterization revealed a rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity and levoglucosan concentrations over time, indicating the degradation of primary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and sugar markers. This dynamic evolution of "brown carbon" absorptivity should be accounted for in radiative forcing models to more accurately assess the climate impact of biomass combustion.
